1:
"And there came one of the seven angels which had the seven vials, and talked with me, saying unto me, Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore that sitteth upon many waters:" The witnesses note the angel offering to show the judgment of the great whore. On "one of the seven angels which had the seven vials... saying, Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore," the grammatical voices note the new vision. Barnes and Robertson note the invitation: "one of the seven angels which had the seven vials... Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore" — one of the vial-angels invites John to see the judgment of "the great whore"; the vision now elaborates the fall of Babylon (16:19) by portraying her as a great harlot under judgment.

On "the great whore," interpreters note the figure. Barnes and Gill note that "the great whore (harlot)" is the symbolic figure of the great corrupt power, Babylon (v.5); the harlot-image marks her spiritual fornication — idolatry, false worship, and corrupting alliance with the world; the historicists read her as papal Rome/the apostate church, others as pagan Rome; the figure of unfaithfulness to God.

On "that sitteth upon many waters," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede and the grammatical-historical note the waters: the whore "sitteth upon many waters" — explained later (v.15) as peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues; she sits enthroned over many peoples; her dominion and influence extend over the nations; Victorinus and Bede read the whore as the great city/power ruling over the nations. The verse displays the angel offering to show the judgment of the great whore.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the invitation — "Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore that sitteth upon many waters," the angel inviting John to see the harlot's judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "the great whore" the corrupt power Babylon, papal Rome to historicists (v.5, Barnes, Gill); "sitteth upon many waters" enthroned over the peoples (v.15, Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the harlot's judgment. The verse marks the angel: of the vials. The verse displays the offer: to show the judgment. The verse displays the figure: the great whore. The verse displays the seat: many waters. The vision displays the corrupt power judged, enthroned over the nations. The verse displays the angel: inviting. The verse displays the whore: great. The verse displays the waters: many. The angel offering to show the judgment of the great whore displays the opening of the vision of Babylon's doom: a vial-angel invites John to see the judgment of the great whore who sits on many waters — the corrupt power (Babylon) enthroned over the peoples, now brought to judgment. The verse displays the great whore, with whom the kings committed fornication (v.2).
2:
"With whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication, and the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication." The brief verse notes the kings and earth-dwellers corrupted by the whore. The grammatical voices note her seduction. Barnes and Robertson note the harlot's corrupting influence: "with whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication, and the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication" — the kings of the earth have committed fornication with the whore, and the earth-dwellers have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication; her corrupting influence has seduced both rulers and peoples into her idolatry and false worship.

On "the kings of the earth have committed fornication," the witnesses note the rulers' corruption. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the kings of the earth have committed fornication" with her — the rulers have entered into corrupt alliance with the harlot-power, sharing her idolatry and worldly corruption; the spiritual fornication of the kings with Babylon.

On "the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the intoxicated peoples: the earth-dwellers "have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication" — the peoples are intoxicated, stupefied, and seduced by her corrupting influence (14:8); her false worship and luxury have made the nations drunk; the universal seduction of the world by the harlot-power. The verse displays the kings and earth-dwellers corrupted by the whore.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the seduction — "with whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication, and the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication," the kings and peoples seduced by the harlot's corruption (Barnes, Robertson); "the kings of the earth have committed fornication," the rulers in corrupt alliance with her (Barnes, Ellicott); "the inhabitants... made drunk with the wine of her fornication," the peoples intoxicated by her influence (14:8, Cyril). The verse displays the seducing whore. The verse marks the kings: fornicated. The verse displays the peoples: made drunk. The verse displays the wine: of her fornication. The verse displays the seduction: universal. The verse displays the harlot corrupting rulers and nations. The verse displays the kings: seduced. The verse displays the earth-dwellers: drunk. The verse displays the fornication: spread. The kings and earth-dwellers corrupted by the whore display the extent of her seduction: the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the inhabitants of the earth are made drunk with the wine of her fornication — rulers and peoples alike seduced by the harlot-power's corrupting influence. The verse displays the seducing whore, seen seated on the scarlet beast (v.3).
3:
"So he carried me away in the spirit into the wilderness: and I saw a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns." The witnesses note the woman seated on the scarlet beast. On "he carried me away in the spirit into the wilderness: and I saw a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast," the grammatical voices note the vision. Barnes and Robertson note the scene: "he carried me away in the spirit into the wilderness: and I saw a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast" — John is carried in the Spirit to a wilderness and sees a woman (the harlot) seated on a scarlet beast; the woman's sitting on the beast marks her alliance with and support by the persecuting power (the beast of ch. 13); the scarlet color marks luxury and bloodshed.

On "a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy," the witnesses note the beast. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy" is the same beast as ch. 13 (the persecuting world-power); scarlet marks its imperial pomp and blood-guilt; "full of names of blasphemy" marks its blasphemous titles and claims; the harlot rides and is upheld by this blasphemous power.

On "having seven heads and ten horns," the grammatical-historical note the marks of the beast: the beast has "seven heads and ten horns" (as 13:1; 12:3) — the symbols explained later (vv.9-12) as seven mountains/kings and ten kings; the harlot is supported by the beast with its heads and horns, the antichristian world-power. The verse displays the woman seated on the scarlet beast.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vision — "a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy," the harlot seated on the persecuting beast (ch. 13), upheld by it (Barnes, Robertson); "scarlet... full of names of blasphemy" the beast's imperial pomp, bloodguilt, and blasphemy (Barnes, Ellicott); "seven heads and ten horns" the beast's marks, explained in vv.9-12 (13:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the beast-borne whore. The verse marks the woman: on the beast. The verse displays the beast: scarlet, blasphemous. The verse displays the heads: seven. The verse displays the horns: ten. The vision displays the harlot upheld by the antichristian power. The verse displays the woman: seated. The verse displays the beast: scarlet. The verse displays the blasphemy: full. The woman seated on the scarlet beast displays the alliance of the harlot and the beast: John sees the woman seated on a scarlet beast full of blasphemous names, with seven heads and ten horns — the corrupt power upheld and carried by the blasphemous, persecuting world-power. The verse displays the beast-borne whore, arrayed in luxury (v.4).
4:
"And the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls, having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication:" The witnesses note the whore's luxurious array and the golden cup of abominations. On "the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls," the grammatical voices note her opulence. Barnes and Robertson note the harlot's array: "the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls" — the whore is clothed in royal and costly colors (purple and scarlet) and adorned with gold, jewels, and pearls; the figure of worldly pomp, luxury, wealth, and splendor; the seductive magnificence of the harlot-power; in the historicist reading, the pomp and wealth of corrupt Rome.

On "purple and scarlet... gold and precious stones and pearls," the witnesses note the seductive splendor. Barnes and Ellicott note that the costly array marks her allurement and worldly glory; she is decked to seduce; the outward magnificence conceals inward corruption (the cup).

On "having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication," the grammatical-historical note the cup of abominations: she holds "a golden cup... full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication" — the golden cup, outwardly attractive, is full of abominations and the filth of her fornication; the contrast between the golden vessel and its foul contents marks the hypocrisy of the harlot — fair without, corrupt within; the cup with which she intoxicates the nations (vv.2; Jeremiah 51:7). The verse displays the whore's luxurious array and the golden cup of abominations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the array — "arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls," the harlot's worldly pomp and seductive splendor (Barnes, Robertson); the costly array her allurement and glory, concealing corruption (Barnes, Ellicott); "a golden cup... full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication," the fair vessel full of foul corruption, her hypocrisy (Jeremiah 51:7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the decked whore. The verse marks the array: purple and scarlet. The verse displays the adornment: gold and jewels. The verse displays the cup: golden. The verse displays the contents: abominations. The figure displays the harlot splendid without, corrupt within. The verse displays the woman: arrayed. The verse displays the cup: held. The verse displays the filth: within. The whore's luxurious array and the golden cup of abominations display her seductive yet corrupt nature: arrayed in purple and scarlet, decked with gold, jewels, and pearls, she holds a golden cup full of abominations and the filthiness of her fornication — splendid without, foul within. The verse displays the decked whore, named MYSTERY BABYLON (v.5).
5:
"And upon her forehead was a name written, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH." The witnesses note the name on the whore's forehead — the crux of her identity. On "upon her forehead was a name written, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT," the grammatical voices note the inscription. Barnes and Robertson note the name: "upon her forehead was a name written, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT" — the harlot bears a name on her forehead (as harlots wore their names); "MYSTERY" marks the name as symbolic, to be understood spiritually, not literally; "BABYLON THE GREAT" identifies her with the great corrupt power.

On the identity of Babylon, interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes (with the Reformed Gill) reads "Babylon the Great" as papal Rome — "the nominal head of the Christian world, as Babylon had been of the heathen world"; the apostate church, the corrupt religious power seated on seven hills (v.9); the historicist identification. The preterist reads Babylon as pagan Rome (the persecuting imperial city); others as the apostate world-system or false religion in general; the name "Babylon" marks her as the seat of idolatry, corruption, and opposition to God (as ancient Babylon).

On "THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH," the grammatical-historical note her title: she is "THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH" — the source and chief of all spiritual fornication and abomination; the fountainhead of idolatry and corruption from which other harlotries spring; the mother of the earth's spiritual whoredoms. The verse displays the name on the whore's forehead.

The witnesses agree the substance: the harlot bears on her forehead a symbolic name — "MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS" — marking her as the great corrupt power, the fountainhead of the earth's spiritual fornication (Barnes, Robertson, Gill). Her identity bears a crux: papal Rome/the apostate church (historicist — Barnes, Gill) vs pagan Rome (preterist) vs the apostate world-system. The verse displays the Babylon crux. The verse marks the name: on her forehead. The verse displays the mystery: symbolic. The verse displays the debate: papal or pagan Rome. The verse displays the title: mother of harlots. The name displays the great corrupt power identified. The verse displays the Babylon: great. The verse displays the mystery: named. The verse displays the harlots: mothered. The name on the whore's forehead displays the unveiling of her identity: "MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH" — the great corrupt power, read by historicists as papal Rome/the apostate church (Barnes, Gill) and by preterists as pagan Rome, the fountainhead of the earth's spiritual fornication. The verse displays the named Babylon, drunk with the blood of the saints (v.6).
6:
"And I saw the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus: and when I saw her, I wondered with great admiration." The witnesses note the whore drunk with the blood of the saints. On "I saw the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus," the grammatical voices note her bloodguilt. Barnes and Robertson note the harlot's persecution: "I saw the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus" — the whore is drunk with the blood of the saints and martyrs; she has shed so much of their blood that she is intoxicated with it; the figure of a great persecuting power, guilty of the blood of God's people; in the historicist reading, the persecutions of corrupt Rome.

On "the blood of the saints, and... the martyrs of Jesus," the witnesses note her crime. Barnes and Gill note that the harlot is guilty of "the blood of the saints, and... the martyrs of Jesus" — those put to death for their faith; her bloodguilt is the killing of the faithful witnesses of Christ; the great persecutor of the church.

On "when I saw her, I wondered with great admiration," the grammatical-historical note John's astonishment: "when I saw her, I wondered with great admiration (amazement)" — John was greatly astonished at the sight; whether amazed at her splendor, her bloodguilt, or the mystery of the vision; his wonder occasions the angel's explanation (v.7); the astonishing spectacle of the blood-drunk harlot in splendor. The verse displays the whore drunk with the blood of the saints.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bloodguilt — "the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus," the harlot intoxicated with the blood of God's people, a great persecutor (Barnes, Robertson); "the blood of the saints, and... the martyrs of Jesus," her crime the killing of the faithful (Barnes, Gill); "I wondered with great admiration," John's astonishment at the sight (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blood-drunk whore. The verse marks the woman: drunken. The verse displays the blood: of the saints. The verse displays the martyrs: of Jesus. The verse displays the wonder: John's amazement. The figure displays the harlot guilty of the martyrs' blood. The verse displays the whore: blood-drunk. The verse displays the saints: slain. The verse displays the wonder: great. The whore drunk with the blood of the saints displays her bloodguilt as persecutor: John sees the woman drunk with the blood of the saints and the martyrs of Jesus, and wonders with great amazement — the splendid harlot intoxicated with the blood of God's people. The verse displays the blood-drunk whore, whose mystery the angel explains (v.7).
7:
"And the angel said unto me, Wherefore didst thou marvel? I will tell thee the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carrieth her, which hath the seven heads and ten horns." The brief verse notes the angel's offer to explain the mystery of the woman and beast. The grammatical voices note the promised explanation. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's words: "Wherefore didst thou marvel? I will tell thee the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carrieth her, which hath the seven heads and ten horns" — the angel, seeing John's amazement (v.6), promises to explain the mystery (the hidden, symbolic meaning) of the woman and the beast that carries her; the interpretation of the vision follows (vv.8-18).

On "the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carrieth her," the witnesses note the things to be explained. Barnes and Henry note that the angel will unfold "the mystery of the woman (the harlot) and of the beast that carrieth her" — both the harlot and the beast that supports her are to be explained; the symbolic meaning of the seven heads and ten horns is now disclosed.

On "which hath the seven heads and ten horns," the grammatical-historical note the beast's marks: the beast "hath the seven heads and ten horns" — the symbols whose meaning the angel now explains (vv.9-12): the seven heads (mountains/kings) and the ten horns (kings); the explanation of the beast's features unlocks the vision. The verse displays the angel's offer to explain the mystery of the woman and beast.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "I will tell thee the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carrieth her," the angel promising to explain the symbolic vision (Barnes, Robertson); "the mystery of the woman, and of the beast" both harlot and beast to be unfolded (Barnes, Henry); "which hath the seven heads and ten horns," the symbols now to be explained (vv.9-12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised explanation. The verse marks the marvel: questioned. The verse displays the offer: I will tell the mystery. The verse displays the subjects: woman and beast. The verse displays the marks: seven heads, ten horns. The angel displays the mystery about to be unfolded. The verse displays the mystery: promised. The verse displays the woman: and beast. The verse displays the explanation: coming. The angel's offer to explain the mystery of the woman and beast displays the turn to interpretation: the angel asks why John marvels and promises to tell the mystery of the woman and of the beast that carries her, with its seven heads and ten horns — the symbolic vision now to be unfolded. The verse displays the promised explanation, beginning with the beast (v.8).
8:
"The beast that thou sawest was, and is not; and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit, and go into perdition: and they that dwell on the earth shall wonder, whose names were not written in the book of life from the foundation of the world, when they behold the beast that was, and is not, and yet is." The witnesses note the beast that "was, and is not, and yet is." On "the beast that thou sawest was, and is not; and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit, and go into perdition," the grammatical voices note the enigmatic description. Barnes and Robertson note the riddle: "the beast that thou sawest was, and is not; and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit, and go into perdition" — the beast is described as one that "was, and is not, and yet is/shall ascend"; it existed, then seemed to pass away, then rises again from the abyss, only to go into destruction; interpreters apply it to the fall and revival of the persecuting power (13:3); the preterist reads the Nero-redivivus expectation; the historicist, the decline and revival of the antichristian power; its end is perdition.

On "was, and is not... and yet is," the witnesses note the paradox. Barnes and Gill note that the beast "was, and is not, and yet is (shall be present)" — the paradoxical existence: it had been, then was not (in some sense fallen or eclipsed), yet is/will be again; the mysterious survival and revival of the persecuting power, doomed at last to perdition.

On "they that dwell on the earth shall wonder, whose names were not written in the book of life," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the wondering world: the earth-dwellers "whose names were not written in the book of life from the foundation of the world" shall "wonder... when they behold the beast" — the unregenerate world, not written in the book of life, marvels at the beast's revival (13:3, 8); only the elect are not deceived; the beast's apparent resurrection draws the world's wondering allegiance. The verse displays the beast that "was, and is not, and yet is."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the riddle — "the beast... was, and is not; and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit, and go into perdition," the persecuting power's fall, revival from the abyss, and doom (13:3, Nero-redivivus to preterists, the antichristian power to historicists, Barnes, Robertson); "was, and is not, and yet is," the paradoxical survival and revival (Barnes, Gill); "they that dwell on the earth shall wonder, whose names were not written in the book of life," the unregenerate world marveling at the beast, only the elect undeceived (13:8, Cyril). The verse displays the reviving beast. The verse marks the beast: was, is not, yet is. The verse displays the ascent: from the abyss. The verse displays the end: perdition. The verse displays the wonder: of the lost. The riddle displays the persecuting power revived and doomed. The verse displays the beast: paradoxical. The verse displays the abyss: its source. The verse displays the perdition: its end. The beast that "was, and is not, and yet is" displays the enigmatic description of the persecuting power: it was, is not, ascends from the abyss, and goes to perdition, while the lost world wonders at it — the fallen and revived antichristian power, doomed at last to destruction. The verse displays the reviving beast, whose seven heads the angel explains (vv.9-11).
9:
"And here is the mind which hath wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth." The brief verse notes the seven heads as seven mountains. The grammatical voices note the call for wisdom and the explanation. Barnes and Robertson note the interpretation: "here is the mind which hath wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth" — the angel invites discerning wisdom (as 13:18), and explains the seven heads as "seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth"; the seven mountains/hills on which the harlot is enthroned.

On "here is the mind which hath wisdom," the witnesses note the call to discern. Barnes and Ellicott note that "here is the mind which hath wisdom" invites the wise to understand the symbol; the explanation requires spiritual discernment to apply rightly.

On "the seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth," the grammatical-historical note the seven hills of Rome: the seven heads "are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth" — almost universally taken as the seven hills of Rome (the city famously built on seven hills); the harlot enthroned on seven mountains points unmistakably to Rome; the historicists, preterists, and most interpreters agree the seven mountains identify the city as Rome; (the heads also signify seven kings, v.10). The verse displays the seven heads as seven mountains.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the explanation — "here is the mind which hath wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth," the call to discern and the explanation of the seven heads (Barnes, Robertson); "here is the mind which hath wisdom" inviting spiritual discernment (13:18, Barnes, Ellicott); "seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth," the seven hills of Rome, identifying the city (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the seven mountains. The verse marks the wisdom: invited. The verse displays the heads: seven mountains. The verse displays the seat: the woman's. The verse displays the identity: Rome's seven hills. The explanation displays the harlot enthroned on Rome's seven hills. The verse displays the heads: explained. The verse displays the mountains: seven. The verse displays the woman: enthroned. The seven heads as seven mountains display the first explanation: with a call for wisdom, the angel reveals that the seven heads are seven mountains on which the woman sits — the seven hills of Rome, identifying the harlot's seat. The verse displays the seven mountains, which are also seven kings (v.10).
10:
"And there are seven kings: five are fallen, and one is, and the other is not yet come; and when he cometh, he must continue a short space." The witnesses note the seven kings — the crux of their meaning. On "there are seven kings: five are fallen, and one is, and the other is not yet come," interpreters divide over the seven kings. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads the seven kings (or heads) as seven successive forms of Roman government or dynasties — "dynasties or forms of government that had ceased to be" (kings, consuls, dictators, decemvirs, military tribunes, emperors, etc.); "five are fallen" (past forms), "one is" (the form then existing), "the other is not yet come" (a form yet future). The preterist reads them as seven Roman emperors — counting from Augustus (or Julius): five fallen (the dead emperors), one is (the reigning emperor at the time of writing), one to come (the next); the historicist reading takes forms of government, the preterist takes individual emperors.

On "five are fallen, and one is, and the other is not yet come," the witnesses note the succession. Barnes and Ellicott note that the sequence (five past, one present, one future) places the time of writing at the sixth head; the identification of the sixth (the then-reigning power or emperor) is the key to dating; the seven represent a succession of ruling powers.

On "when he cometh, he must continue a short space," the grammatical-historical note the brief seventh: "when he cometh, he must continue a short space" — the seventh king/form, when it comes, lasts only a short time; its brevity is noted; the seventh is transient, giving way to the eighth (the beast, v.11). The verse displays the seven kings.

The witnesses agree the substance: the seven heads are also seven kings, in succession — five fallen, one present, one yet to come (briefly) — placing the time of writing at the sixth (Barnes, Robertson). Their identity bears a crux: seven forms/dynasties of Roman government (historicist — Barnes) vs seven individual Roman emperors (preterist). The verse displays the seven-kings crux. The verse marks the kings: seven. The verse displays the sequence: five fallen, one is, one to come. The verse displays the debate: forms of government or emperors. The verse displays the seventh: brief. The explanation displays the succession of ruling powers. The verse displays the kings: succeeding. The verse displays the present: the sixth. The verse displays the seventh: short. The seven kings display a famous crux of interpretation: the seven heads are seven kings — five fallen, one is, one not yet come (and brief) — read by historicists as successive forms or dynasties of Roman government (Barnes) and by preterists as individual Roman emperors. The verse displays the seven kings, with the beast as the eighth (v.11).
11:
"And the beast that was, and is not, even he is the eighth, and is of the seven, and goeth into perdition." The brief verse notes the beast as the eighth king, of the seven. The grammatical voices note the enigmatic eighth. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "the beast that was, and is not, even he is the eighth, and is of the seven, and goeth into perdition" — the beast (the persecuting power, v.8) is the "eighth" king, yet "of the seven" (springing from them, of the same kind); it is a continuation and culmination of the seven, yet distinct as the eighth; its end, like the beast's (v.8), is perdition.

On "the eighth, and is of the seven," the witnesses note the paradox. Barnes and Ellicott note that the beast "is the eighth, and is of the seven" — both an eighth power and one of the seven; the revived beast (the persecuting power in its final or revived form) is reckoned as an eighth, yet belongs to the same series; the preterist reads a returning emperor (Nero redivivus) as the eighth; the historicist, the final form of the antichristian power.

On "goeth into perdition," the grammatical-historical note the doom: the beast "goeth into perdition" — its end is destruction; however it revives or culminates, the beast is doomed to perish; the certain overthrow of the persecuting power; the repetition (v.8) stresses its appointed ruin. The verse displays the beast as the eighth king, of the seven.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the eighth — "the beast that was, and is not, even he is the eighth, and is of the seven," the beast both an eighth power and of the seven, the culmination of the persecuting power (Barnes, Robertson); "the eighth, and is of the seven," the revived beast reckoned as eighth yet of the series, a returning emperor to preterists, the final antichristian form to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "goeth into perdition," its appointed doom (v.8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eighth beast. The verse marks the beast: the eighth. The verse displays the origin: of the seven. The verse displays the paradox: was, is not. The verse displays the end: perdition. The explanation displays the beast as the doomed culmination of the seven. The verse displays the eighth: reckoned. The verse displays the seven: its kind. The verse displays the perdition: its end. The beast as the eighth king, of the seven, displays the culmination of the persecuting power: the beast that was and is not is the eighth, yet of the seven, and goes into perdition — the revived antichristian power, both eighth and of the series, doomed to destruction. The verse displays the doomed beast, with its ten horns the ten kings (v.12).
12:
"And the ten horns which thou sawest are ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet; but receive power as kings one hour with the beast." The brief verse notes the ten horns as ten kings allied with the beast. The grammatical voices note the explanation. Barnes and Robertson note the interpretation: "the ten horns which thou sawest are ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet; but receive power as kings one hour with the beast" — the ten horns are ten kings who had not yet received their kingdom (at the time of the vision), but receive royal power "one hour" (for a short time) together with the beast; the kings allied with and subject to the beast for a brief period.

On "ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet," the witnesses note their future rise. Barnes and Ellicott note that the ten kings "have received no kingdom as yet" — they were yet future when John wrote; in the historicist reading, the kingdoms/powers that arose out of the divided Roman empire (the ten kingdoms of the broken empire); they rise to share power with the beast.

On "receive power as kings one hour with the beast," the grammatical-historical note the brief allied rule: the ten kings "receive power as kings one hour (a short time) with the beast" — their authority is brief and exercised in alliance with the beast; they give their power to the beast (v.13) and war with the Lamb (v.14); the short-lived confederacy of kings supporting the antichristian power. The verse displays the ten horns as ten kings allied with the beast.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the explanation — "the ten horns... are ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet; but receive power as kings one hour with the beast," the ten kings allied with the beast for a brief time (Barnes, Robertson); "received no kingdom as yet" their future rise, the kingdoms of the divided empire to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "power as kings one hour with the beast," their brief allied rule (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ten kings. The verse marks the horns: ten kings. The verse displays the timing: not yet reigning. The verse displays the power: one hour. The verse displays the alliance: with the beast. The explanation displays the ten kings briefly allied with the beast. The verse displays the horns: explained. The verse displays the kings: ten. The verse displays the hour: short. The ten horns as ten kings allied with the beast display the explanation of the horns: the ten horns are ten kings who, not yet reigning, receive power for one hour with the beast — the short-lived confederacy of kings supporting the antichristian power. The verse displays the ten kings, who give their power to the beast (v.13).
13:
"These have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast." The very brief verse notes the ten kings giving their power to the beast. The grammatical voices note their united purpose. Barnes and Robertson note the confederacy: "these have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast" — the ten kings are of one mind (united in purpose) and give their power and strength to the beast; they place their authority and resources at the service of the antichristian power; the united support of the kings for the beast.

On "these have one mind," the witnesses note their unity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the ten kings "have one mind" — they are agreed, united in a common purpose to support the beast; their concord serves the antichristian cause.

On "shall give their power and strength unto the beast," the grammatical-historical note the surrendered authority: the kings "shall give their power and strength unto the beast" — they surrender their authority and might to the beast, becoming its instruments; the concentration of the kings' power in the beast for the war against the Lamb (v.14); the willing subordination of the kings to the antichristian power. The verse displays the ten kings giving their power to the beast.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the confederacy — "these have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast," the united kings surrendering their power to the beast (Barnes, Robertson); "these have one mind" their agreed, united purpose (Barnes, Ellicott); "shall give their power and strength unto the beast," the kings becoming the beast's instruments (v.14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the united kings. The verse marks the mind: one. The verse displays the gift: power and strength. The verse displays the recipient: the beast. The verse displays the purpose: united support. The confederacy displays the kings surrendering their power to the beast. The verse displays the kings: united. The verse displays the power: given. The verse displays the beast: served. The ten kings giving their power to the beast display the united confederacy: the ten kings are of one mind and give their power and strength to the beast — surrendering their authority to the antichristian power for the coming war. The verse displays the united kings, who war with the Lamb (v.14).
14:
"These shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them: for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings: and they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful." The witnesses note the war with the Lamb and the Lamb's victory. On "these shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them," the grammatical voices note the conflict and its outcome. Barnes and Robertson note the war: "these shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them" — the beast and the ten kings make war against the Lamb (Christ), but the Lamb overcomes them; the climactic conflict between the antichristian powers and Christ ends in Christ's victory; the assured triumph of the Lamb over all his enemies.

On "for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings," the witnesses note the ground of victory. Barnes and Henry note the reason: "for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings" — the Lamb conquers because he is supreme over all; his title (19:16) marks his sovereign supremacy over all kings and powers; the kings and beast cannot prevail against the King of kings.

On "they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the Lamb's followers: "they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful" — those who share his victory are the called, chosen, and faithful (his elect and loyal people); the threefold description marks the true saints who accompany the Lamb; they triumph with him. The verse displays the war with the Lamb and the Lamb's victory.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the war — "these shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them," the antichristian powers warring on Christ and overcome (Barnes, Robertson); "for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings," the Lamb's sovereign supremacy the ground of victory (19:16, Barnes, Henry); "they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful," the elect and loyal saints who triumph with him (Henry). The verse displays the Lamb's victory. The verse marks the war: with the Lamb. The verse displays the outcome: the Lamb overcomes. The verse displays the title: Lord of lords. The verse displays the followers: called, chosen, faithful. The conflict displays the Lamb triumphant over the antichristian powers. The verse displays the war: waged. The verse displays the Lamb: victorious. The verse displays the saints: faithful. The war with the Lamb and the Lamb's victory display the assured triumph of Christ: the beast and kings make war on the Lamb, but the Lamb overcomes them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings, and those with him are called, chosen, and faithful. The verse displays the victorious Lamb, before the harlot's destruction by the beast and kings (vv.15-16).
15:
"And he saith unto me, The waters which thou sawest, where the whore sitteth, are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues." The brief verse notes the waters explained as peoples and nations. The grammatical voices note the interpretation. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's explanation: "the waters which thou sawest, where the whore sitteth, are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues" — the "many waters" (v.1) on which the harlot sits are explained as peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues; the whore is enthroned over and rules the masses of mankind; her dominion extends over the nations.

On "the waters... are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues," the witnesses note her dominion. Barnes and Ellicott note that the waters signify "peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues" — the fourfold designation of the whole mass of mankind; the harlot's seat on the waters marks her rule and influence over all peoples; the universal extent of her corrupting sway.

On the figure, the puritan Henry notes the meaning: the waters as peoples shows that the harlot's power and seduction reach the multitudes of nations; her dominion is over the populous world; the explanation confirms her as a great power ruling the peoples (consistent with v.1). The verse displays the waters explained as peoples and nations.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the explanation — "the waters which thou sawest, where the whore sitteth, are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues," the waters explained as the masses of mankind over whom the harlot rules (Barnes, Robertson); "peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues" the universal extent of her sway (Barnes, Ellicott); the harlot's dominion over the populous world (Henry). The verse displays her dominion over the peoples. The verse marks the waters: explained. The verse displays the meaning: peoples and nations. The verse displays the seat: over the multitudes. The verse displays the sway: universal. The explanation displays the harlot enthroned over all peoples. The verse displays the waters: peoples. The verse displays the nations: ruled. The verse displays the dominion: wide. The waters explained as peoples and nations display the extent of the harlot's rule: the waters where the whore sits are peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues — her dominion and corrupting influence over the masses of mankind. The verse displays the harlot's dominion, soon turned against her by the kings (v.16).
16:
"And the ten horns which thou sawest upon the beast, these shall hate the whore, and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire." The witnesses note the ten kings turning on and destroying the whore. On "the ten horns which thou sawest upon the beast, these shall hate the whore," the grammatical voices note the turning. Barnes and Robertson note the reversal: "the ten horns... upon the beast, these shall hate the whore, and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire" — the ten kings (and the beast) shall turn against the harlot they once supported; they hate her, strip her, devour her flesh, and burn her with fire; the powers that upheld Babylon become the instruments of her destruction.

On "make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh," the witnesses note the savage destruction. Barnes and Ellicott note that the kings "make her desolate and naked (strip her of her wealth and glory), and shall eat her flesh (devour her, ruin her utterly)" — the total despoiling and destruction of the harlot by her former allies; the figure of a complete and violent overthrow.

On "burn her with fire," the grammatical-historical and Reformed JFB note the burning: "burn her with fire" — the harlot is burned (as adulteresses and the condemned were burned, Leviticus 21:9); the burning of Babylon (18:8); the fitting and final destruction of the corrupt city by the very powers that had served her; in the historicist reading, the turning of the nations/kings against corrupt Rome. The verse displays the ten kings turning on and destroying the whore.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turning — "the ten horns... these shall hate the whore, and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire," the kings turning against and destroying the harlot they once upheld (Barnes, Robertson); "make her desolate and naked... eat her flesh" the total despoiling and ruin (Barnes, Ellicott); "burn her with fire," the burning of Babylon by her former allies (18:8, JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the destroyed whore. The verse marks the kings: hating her. The verse displays the stripping: desolate and naked. The verse displays the devouring: her flesh. The verse displays the burning: with fire. The reversal displays Babylon destroyed by her own allies. The verse displays the horns: turned. The verse displays the whore: ruined. The verse displays the fire: burning. The ten kings turning on and destroying the whore display the overthrow of Babylon: the ten horns and the beast hate the harlot, make her desolate and naked, devour her flesh, and burn her with fire — the powers that upheld her becoming the instruments of her destruction. The verse displays the destroyed whore, by God's overruling purpose (v.17).
17:
"For God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will, and to agree, and give their kingdom unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled." The brief verse notes God's overruling purpose in the destruction of the whore. The grammatical voices note the divine sovereignty. Barnes and Robertson note the explanation: "God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will, and to agree, and give their kingdom unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled" — the kings' turning on the harlot, their agreement, and their giving their kingdom to the beast are all by God's overruling providence; God puts it in their hearts to fulfill his will; even the wicked powers unwittingly serve God's purpose until his words are accomplished.

On "God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will," the witnesses note the divine overruling. Barnes and Ellicott note that "God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will" — God so directs even the malice and agreement of the wicked kings that they accomplish his purpose (the destruction of Babylon); the sovereignty of God over the actions of his enemies; they fulfill his will even in their wickedness.

On "until the words of God shall be fulfilled," the grammatical-historical note the appointed limit: the kings serve the beast "until the words of God shall be fulfilled" — their alliance and actions continue until God's prophetic words are accomplished; God's purpose sets the term; when his words are fulfilled, the antichristian powers have served their appointed end and are themselves destroyed. The verse displays God's overruling purpose in the destruction of the whore.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sovereignty — "God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will, and to agree, and give their kingdom unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled," the kings unwittingly serving God's purpose by his overruling (Barnes, Robertson); "God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will," God directing even the wicked to accomplish his purpose (Barnes, Ellicott); "until the words of God shall be fulfilled," their actions bounded by God's appointed term (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the overruled kings. The verse marks the hearts: moved by God. The verse displays the will: fulfilled. The verse displays the kingdom: given to the beast. The verse displays the limit: God's words fulfilled. The explanation displays God overruling the wicked for his purpose. The verse displays the kings: directed. The verse displays the will: God's. The verse displays the words: fulfilled. God's overruling purpose in the destruction of the whore displays the divine sovereignty behind the events: God puts it in the kings' hearts to fulfill his will, to agree and give their kingdom to the beast, until his words are fulfilled — even the wicked powers unwittingly serving God's purpose. The verse displays the overruled kings, and the identification of the woman as the great city (v.18).
18:
"And the woman which thou sawest is that great city, which reigneth over the kings of the earth." The witnesses note the woman identified as the great city reigning over the kings. On "the woman which thou sawest is that great city," the grammatical voices note the final identification. Barnes and Robertson note the explicit naming: "the woman which thou sawest is that great city, which reigneth over the kings of the earth" — the angel concludes the explanation by identifying the woman (the harlot) as "that great city" — Babylon (v.5), the great city that reigns over the kings of the earth; the harlot is a city/power exercising dominion over the world's rulers.

On "that great city," interpreters note the reference. Barnes and Gill note that "that great city" is Babylon (16:19; 17:5) — read by the historicists as Rome (papal Rome, the city on seven hills, v.9), the seat of the corrupt power that reigned over the kings; the preterist reads pagan Rome (the imperial city that ruled the world); the great city that dominates the earth's rulers.

On "which reigneth over the kings of the earth," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note her dominion: the great city "reigneth over the kings of the earth" — she exercises sovereignty over the world's rulers; her dominion over the kings marks her as the great ruling power (Rome, the mistress of nations); the harlot-city's rule over the kings of the earth, now doomed to destruction. The verse displays the woman identified as the great city reigning over the kings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the identification — "the woman which thou sawest is that great city, which reigneth over the kings of the earth," the harlot identified as the great city (Babylon) ruling the world's kings (16:19; 17:5, Barnes, Robertson); "that great city" Babylon, Rome to historicists/preterists (v.9, Barnes, Gill); "which reigneth over the kings of the earth," her dominion over the rulers, the mistress of nations (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great city. The verse marks the woman: the great city. The verse displays the identity: Babylon. The verse displays the dominion: over the kings. The verse displays the reference: Rome. The identification displays the harlot as the great ruling city. The verse displays the woman: named. The verse displays the city: great. The verse displays the kings: ruled. The woman identified as the great city reigning over the kings displays the conclusion of the explanation: the woman is that great city which reigns over the kings of the earth — Babylon (read as Rome), the great corrupt power dominating the world's rulers, now doomed to destruction. The verse displays the great city, whose fall is lamented and celebrated (ch.18).
